title: Break 90 Circular Redirect Loops; Root Blog Posts (Priority Batch)
description: Screaming Frog confirmed 90 circular redirect loops where /[slug]/ redirects to /blog/[slug]/ which redirects back to /[slug]/. This is the primary cause of the 96% keyword ranking collapse. Resolution: For each affected post, identify the dominant URL version (based on GSC impressions), ensure that version returns 200, and ensure the other version returns a clean 301 pointing to it not back. Priority loop fixes (keep /blog/ version): oil-to-gas guide, AC noise post, gas leak post, water pressure guide, tankless water heater, mini-split vs central, sump pump alarms. Priority loop fixes (keep root version): AI smart thermostats, tax credits rebates, annual HVAC maintenance checklist. All remaining loops: default to /blog/[slug]/ as canonical. Verify each fix returns 200 before moving to next.

title: Noindex All Blog Pagination Pages (/blog/page/2/ through /blog/page/15/)
description: 14 blog pagination pages are confirmed indexable with identical title tags ('HVAC Blog | Bruni & Campisi | Learn More'); 14 duplicate titles in Google's index. Pages at crawl depth 4–5 are suppressing crawl budget to legitimate blog posts. Fix: In Yoast SEO, navigate to Search Appearance > Content Types > Posts > Archive Pages > set to noindex. Confirm all /blog/page/N/ URLs return noindex in browser source after saving.

title: 301 Redirect /ductless-hvac/ to /ductless-ac-installation/
description: Screaming Frog confirmed both /ductless-hvac/ and /ductless-ac-installation/ are live, indexable, and share the identical title tag 'Ductless AC Install Elmsford NY | Bruni & Campisi.' This is a confirmed on-site duplicate splitting ductless AC authority. Implement 301 from /ductless-hvac/ → /ductless-ac-installation/. No content changes needed.

title: Internal Linking System
description:
Strategy:
Build contextual linking network


Implementation:
  • Link homepage → all service pages
  • Link service pages → related services
  • Use keyword-rich anchor text
Expected Outcome:
  • Improved crawl prioritization
  • Stronger page authority distribution

title: Fix /rooftop-ac-systems/ 404; 301 Redirect to /central-air-conditioning/
description: The /rooftop-ac-systems/ page returns a 404 but has 529 total internal links (264 unique), the highest broken inlink count in the entire crawl. Every one of those links is leaking authority into a dead end. Implement a 301 redirect from /rooftop-ac-systems/ to /central-air-conditioning/. No content changes needed. Verify in GSC Coverage after implementation.

title: Include Sitemap In footer of Client websites
description:
Hi, this is inline w/ a group of tasks we have from a sheet. Here is the link for reference: https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1qPfUUC1J2r1s7Ot8Y1PmIcje6WpyMszDhxHGz4TxDTk/edit?gid=0#gid=0

SEO added more info column D. 

I have included the task info here:
--- 
Hey Team!

To enhance our technical SEO baseline, improve crawl efficiency, and ensure consistent signaling across all client properties, It would be great to come to an agreement to outlining a new, standardized protocol for crawl management.

My suggestion is as follows:

Implement the following three directives on all client websites as part of our standard build and maintenance process.

1. XML Sitemap in robots.txt (Primary Directive)

This is the most effective way to inform search engines of all indexable URLs.

Action: Ensure the robots.txt file for every client domain contains a Sitemap directive pointing to the full URL of the XML sitemap.

Implementation Example:
User-agent: 
Sitemap: https://www.clientdomain.com/sitemap.xml

The "Why": This provides a clear, direct path for Google to find and crawl our most important pages, ensuring faster discovery and indexing of new content.

2. User-Facing HTML Sitemap (Footer)

This is for user experience and secondary crawl pathing.

Action: All client websites must have a user-facing HTML sitemap.

Implementation: This sitemap must be linked in the footer of every page on the site.

The "Why": This provides a clear navigation path for users, improves site accessibility, and   acts as a secondary crawl path for search engines to discover deep pages.

Strategic Crawl & Indexation Control

This is critical. We must be precise in telling crawlers what to crawl and what to index.

A. To Control Crawling (Using robots.txt)

We must Disallow crawlers from accessing low-value, non-SEO pages to conserve crawl budget.

Action: Audit and update the robots.txt file to Disallow non-public or utility sections of the site.
Implementation Examples:
Disallow: /wp-admin/

The WP sitemap page  plugin in perfect to use as a plugin for now

Regarding providing a robot.txt:

They differ per client especially regarding disallows but here is a comprehensive one that can be edited according to client needs especially regarding disallows:

User-agent: *

# --- Core WordPress File Disallows ---
# Block all core administrative and include folders.
Disallow: /wp-admin/
Disallow: /wp-includes/

# --- Crawl Budget Optimization ---
# Block non-media plugin and theme files from being crawled.
Disallow: /wp-content/plugins/
Disallow: /wp-content/themes/

# Block internal search result pages, which are low-quality, duplicate content.
Disallow: /?s=
Disallow: /search/

# Block other non-public WordPress files and pages.
Disallow: /wp-login.php
Disallow: /readme.html
Disallow: /trackback/
Disallow: /xmlrpc.php

# --- Allow Directives for Critical Files ---
# Explicitly ALLOW critical AJAX and asset files that Google
# may need for rendering, even if their parent folder is disallowed.
Allow: /wp-admin/admin-ajax.php
Allow: /wp-content/uploads/

# --- Sitemap Directive ---
# This is the primary directive to tell all search engines where our XML sitemap is located.
# [IMPORTANT: Replace with the client's actual sitemap URL]
Sitemap: https://www.clientdomain.com/sitemap_index.xml

Also, just an example of placement. Sitemap listed at the very bottom of the footer on the left followed by privacy policy etc.

* In the footer we will use the page /sitemap/ and not the XML sitemap.  The XML sitemap is for Google & the robots.txt.


you can use this as robots.txt:

title: Rather Big Problem With the Site's H-tag Structure
description:
First of all, this is how the SEO META extension sees the meta title structure of the site's service pages and posts.

Sample page:  https://www.bruniandcampisi.com/drain-cleaning/


Sample blog post: https://www.bruniandcampisi.com/blog/furnace-tune-up/


1. All service pages and blog posts have a secondary H1 tag:   <h1>Query Monitor</h1>. This particular H1 and all the other H2  and H3 tags below it, should not appear as H tags at all. Also, this secondary H1 tag, does not appear on the Screaming Frog crawl as a duplicate H1 tag, but can be seen in the page or blog source:


2. All services pages have an accordion element like this:


The titles in it appear as <p></p>. Even though the accordion has this <h2></h2> at the beginning:

I am not sure if all these titles appear as H2 tags, as they are not visible in the SEO META extension at all.

The above is from the https://www.bruniandcampisi.com/drain-cleaning/ page, but all services pages have a similar structure.

They should have clean <h2></h2> tags.

===========================

So, the requests are these:

1. Eliminate all the H-tags that are below <h1>Query Monitor</h1> including it. In other words, eliminate the H-tags on all those sentences or whatever they're called, if possible.


2. Make the titles in the accordion that appears on most service pages appear as H2 tags.

-----------

I hope I formulated this correctly.
